generations.text:
My Dearest Eliza,

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with regret. The wind howls like a banshee, and the sea roars as though it would swallow the very cliffs that guard this lighthouse. I fear the night may claim me before dawn, and if it does, I pray this letter finds you—though I know not how, for I have no means to send it.

Five years have passed since you left, Eliza. Five years of silence, of longing, of sleepless nights where I would wake to the sound of the waves, and I would think of you. I have kept the light burning, as I always have, not for the sailors who pass by, but for you. For the girl who once stood at the edge of the cliff and asked me if the sea ever whispered secrets. I told you it did, and that it spoke of things I could not tell you.

I have not written to you, not because I have no words, but because I feared they would not be enough. I feared they would not be right. I feared you would not want to hear them. And so I let the silence grow, like the fog that rolls in at dusk.

But now, as the storm gathers strength and the waves grow fiercer, I feel the weight of my years pressing upon me. I am not the man I once was, and I fear I may not see the morning. And if I do not, I

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. The sea has been restless tonight, and the wind howls like a beast in pain. I have lit the lamp, as I have done for thirty years, but tonight I fear it may be the last time. The storm is upon us, and I do not know if the light will guide any ships—or if it will guide me home.

Five years have passed since the last letter you sent, and I have not replied. I do not know if you still remember me, or if you have forgotten the man who once held you in his arms. I have been a lighthouse keeper, a solitary soul on this rock, watching the sea with a lantern and a prayer. But I have not been alone. I have been haunted by the sound of your voice, the echo of your laughter, and the weight of your absence.

I have always believed that a lighthouse keeper must be steadfast, unshaken by the storm. That is what I told myself when you left. That is what I told myself when you wrote that you could not stay, that you had to go. I told myself that you were better off without me, that the sea was a cruel mistress and that I had no right to keep you from your own life.

But I have not been able to let go. I have not been able to stop thinking of you. I have not been able

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the sea howling at my window and the wind tearing at the shutters like it seeks to tear down the very walls of this lighthouse. The sky is a bruise of black and green, and the waves are rising like the devil himself is trying to reclaim the shore. I have lit the lamp, as I always do, though I fear the light may not be enough to guide the ships through this maelstrom.

Five years, Eleanor. Five years since you left. Five years since I last heard your voice, or saw your face. Time has not softened the edges of the hurt, nor has it mended the rift between us. I have tried to write to you, more than once, but the letters always went unanswered. I suppose it is not surprising. You were never one for sentiment, and I, for all my years on this lonely rock, have never been good at saying what I mean.

You were a child when you left, no more than twelve, and I, a man who had long since lost the ability to be a father. I had hoped you would come back, that the sea would bring you to me, that the light would guide you home. But the sea is fickle, and so is the heart.

I have lived alone here for so long now, with only the crows for company and the wind to keep me company. I have seen the stars, and the moon,

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the wind howling like the ghosts of those who came before me, and the sea roaring as though it would swallow the very earth. The storm is upon us, and I fear I may not see the dawn. But I must write, for I have not spoken to you in five years, and I fear that if I do not, I may never have the chance again.

You were once my light, Eleanor. Bright as the beacon that stands atop this cliff. I remember the day you left, the way your eyes were full of fire and your voice like a blade. You said I was a fool, that I clung to this lonely post like a man bound to a shipwreck. You were right, in many ways. I did cling. I clung to duty, to the sea, to the silence of the night. And in doing so, I lost you.

I know you believe I never loved you enough, that I never fought for you when you were young and your heart was too tender for the world. But I was a man of stone, Eleanor, and I did not know how to be soft. I did not know how to be a father. I thought that by keeping the light burning, I could keep you safe. But I was wrong. You were not safe. You were lost.

I have spent these years watching the waves, listening to the wind, and wondering if you had found peace

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

It is with a heart heavy as the sea at dusk that I take up my quill. The wind howls like a banshee at my door, and the waves crash with a fury that makes me fear I may not live to see dawn. Yet, in this moment of uncertainty, I find myself compelled to write—to you, my dear Eleanor, my only child.

Five years have passed since we last spoke, and I have not heard a word from you since that last letter you sent, cold and clipped as the autumn air. I do not know what has changed between us, nor do I know if I have the right to hope that anything has. But tonight, as I stand at the helm of this lonely lighthouse, I feel a strange, aching need to reach out to the only one who ever truly understood me.

You were always the light in my life, Eleanor. I remember the nights you would sit by the window, watching the sea with me, and we would speak of the stars and the tides and the dreams we once had. You were the one who taught me that the darkest nights are the ones that need the brightest light the most. I have tried to live by that, even when the world seemed to have forgotten me.

But I fear that the world has not forgotten me. I have grown old, and the sea has grown cruel. The storms have become more frequent, and the lantern has not always burned with

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. Five long years have passed since the last time I laid eyes upon you, and yet the weight of that absence sits upon my soul like a stone. I have not spoken your name in all that time, not even to myself, and I fear that now, as the storm gathers strength beyond the horizon, I may never have the chance to say it again.

The wind howls like a banshee, and the sea roars with a fury that I have not known in my years upon this rock. The lighthouse stands as it always has—solid, steadfast, a beacon to those who might need it. But I fear that tonight, it may not be enough. I have seen the waves, Eliza. They come with a wrath that no man can tame, and I have no illusions that I will survive what is to come. I have spent my life keeping this light burning, but I fear I may not be able to keep it for much longer.

You were always my light, my greatest hope. I remember the day you left—how the sun set upon your back as you walked away, and how I could not bring myself to call after you. I told myself that you were better off without me, that you would find happiness in the world beyond this lonely coast. But I was wrong, my child. I have been alone for far too long.

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a beast upon the cliffs, and the sea raging as though it would swallow the very rocks on which I stand. The lantern flickers, its light wavering like my own resolve, and I find myself staring into the storm, wondering if I will see another dawn.

Five long years have passed since you left this house, and yet I feel as though the day you walked out of the door was but yesterday. I have not heard from you, nor have I dared to write, fearing the words might not reach you, or worse, that they would find you unprepared for what I have to say. But now, as the night falls and the tempest threatens to break upon us, I find I must speak, if only to the wind.

You were but a child when you left, and I, a man who had long since lost the will to be a father. I told myself it was for your own good, that you would find your own path, your own way in the world. But I have not been able to let go of the thought that I had failed you, that I had not been the man you needed. I have not been the man you deserved.

I have kept the lighthouse, as I always have, not for the glory of it, nor for the pride of it, but because I have no one else to care for this place. The sea

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years unspoken. The sea howls outside my window like a beast in torment, and the wind howls with it, as though the very elements conspire to erase what time has already stolen from us. I do not know if I shall live to see the dawn, but if I do, I must say these words—before they are lost to the sea, as so many things have been.

Five years, Eliza. Five long, bitter years since you left this place, leaving behind not just the lighthouse, but the man I once was. I have not spoken your name aloud in all that time, for fear it might summon you back, and I am not ready to face the ghost of what we were.

You were a child when you ran away, as children do—though I fear I was the one who taught you to run. I was too stern, too wrapped up in the sea and the duty it demanded. I did not see the cracks in the walls you built around your heart, nor did I understand the pain that led you to leave. I thought I was protecting you, but I only pushed you further away.

I have not seen the sea in years, not since you left. I have spent my days alone, the light beam dancing across the dark, a silent witness to the world I once shared with you. I have watched the moon

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a trembling hand, the wind howling like a banshee through the cracks of this old lighthouse. The sea is restless, the sky a canvas of black and fury, and I fear the storm may not be the only thing that will take me. But I must write, for if I do not, I shall be left with the weight of silence and regret.

Five years have passed since you left this place, and I have not heard a word from you. I have tried to write, to send letters, to find some way to reach you, but the post has been unreliable, and your address has long since changed. I suppose you have moved on, as I have tried to do. I have kept this lighthouse, this solitary duty, as a shield against the world. But it is not enough. It never was.

I have always known I was not the kind of father you needed. I was a man of the sea, not of the home. I was a man of duty, not of sentiment. I told myself that the lighthouse was my family, that the light I kept burning was a beacon for others, and that in that, I was doing what was right. But now, as I stand alone in the storm, I wonder if I have been blind to the truth all along.

You were a child when you left, and I was a man too proud to ask for your forgiveness. I have

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a hand that trembles not only from the cold, but from the weight of silence that has stretched between us like the cliffs that guard this lighthouse. Five years, Eleanor. Five long, bitter years since you left, and I have not spoken a word to you. Not a single word. Not even a greeting.

I suppose it is fitting, in a way, that the storm comes tonight — a storm as fierce and unrelenting as the silence that has settled over our lives. I have been watching the sky all evening, the clouds thickening like a shroud, the wind howling as if it knew the truth I have kept locked away.

You were not the first to leave, Eleanor. But you were the first to leave me with no explanation, no goodbye. I have tried to forget you, to let the sea take your memory and wash it away. But the sea is fickle, and it has returned to me with the same tides, the same waves, the same longing.

I have spent these years in this lighthouse, keeping the light burning, not just for the ships, but for the hope that one day, you might return. I have kept the fire alive, even when the world seemed to have forgotten us. Even when I thought you had.

But now, as the storm gathers strength, I fear I may not be here to see the dawn. I have been warned by the old

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I hope this letter finds you in good health, though I fear it may not find you at all. The sea has been restless these past few days, and the wind howls like a wounded beast. I have lit the lamp, as I always do, and though it burns bright, I cannot shake the feeling that it may be the last time I shall do so.

Five years have passed since you left, Eliza. Five years of silence, of long nights and endless waves. I have tried to forget you, but the sea does not forget. It whispers your name in the foam, and the wind carries your voice on the gales. I have seen your face in the mist, in the storm, in the reflection of the moon on the water. I have tried to be strong, but the sea is a cruel mistress, and it has taken much from me.

Tonight, the storm is coming. I have heard the warnings, the fishermen who return from the coast speak of a tempest unlike any in memory. The sky is black as pitch, and the waves rise like mountains. I have prepared the boat, the lantern, the provisions—but I know, in my heart, that I may not make it through the night. The lighthouse has stood for over a century, and I have kept its light burning for more than thirty years. But the sea is not so forgiving.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you in better health than I. I write with trembling hands, for the wind howls like a wounded beast and the sea roars as if it would swallow the very stones of this cliff. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet, as I sit here in the dim glow of the oil lamp, the weight of silence between us is heavier than the storm that now threatens to break upon us.

I have always been a man of few words, Eleanor. Not because I lack feeling, but because I feared they would not be enough. I feared they would be misunderstood, or worse—ignored. You were never one to listen, not when I spoke of the sea, nor when I spoke of my fears, nor when I spoke of you. You were too young then, too caught up in your own world, too busy chasing dreams that would never take you home.

I have watched the years pass, and with them, the dreams you once held. I have seen you grow into a woman of strength and resolve, and though I have not been there to see it, I have felt it in the way the wind carries your name through the air. I have felt it in the way the sea, ever faithful, has whispered your name to me each night as I stood watch.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

It is with a heart heavy with sorrow and a mind clouded by the weight of years unspoken that I take up this quill. The ink is thick, the paper feels coarse beneath my fingers, and the wind howls like a wounded beast against the lighthouse walls. Tonight, a storm is coming—one that has not touched these shores in decades. I fear it may not be kind to the old bones that have stood guard here for so many years.

I have not written to you in five years, Eleanor. Five long, silent years. I have not spoken your name aloud since the day you left, and I have not dared to think of you without the sting of regret. I have tried, in my own way, to keep the light burning, not just for the ships that pass, but for the hope that perhaps, one day, you might return.

I know you must think me a cruel man, a man who turned his back on you when you needed him most. But I have not forgotten. I have not forgotten the day you came to the door, your eyes red with tears, your voice trembling with the weight of a decision I could not bear to hear. You said you were leaving, and I said nothing. I only watched you go, as the waves lapped at the shore and the wind tore at the sails of the ship that carried you away.

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with a hand that trembles not only from the cold, but from the weight of years unspoken and hearts unkind. The wind howls like a beast outside, and the sea, that old, fickle mistress, roars with a fury that makes me wonder if it is the sea or my soul that is truly raging.

Five years since you left, Eliza. Five years since I closed the door behind you, and though I’ve opened it many times since, you never came back. I have not spoken your name in all that time, not even to the waves that crash against the rocks below. But tonight—tonight, the storm is upon us, and I fear I may not see dawn.

I do not write this to beg your forgiveness, for I do not believe I deserve it. You were a child then, and I, a man who thought himself a father, a husband, a keeper of the light. But I was blind, Eliza. Blind to the pain I caused, the silence I let fester, the love I withheld. I was a lighthouse keeper, and I let the light go out.

Five years, Eliza. Five years since you left, and five years since I have spoken your name aloud. I have not written, nor have I dared to think of you. But tonight, as the storm gathers strength and the waves rise like a vengeful tide, I find myself unable to hold back. The silence between us has grown too vast, and I fear it may swallow me whole.

You were a child when you ran from this place, from me, from the life we were meant to share. I remember the day you stood at the door, your eyes like shards of glass, and said, “You don’t understand.” I did not. I could not. I was a man of the sea, of stone and salt, and you were a girl of dreams and distant horizons. You wanted more than the lonely watch of the lighthouse, more than the endless nights and the cold wind that bit through the cracks in the walls.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I daresay I have no right to hope for such a thing. Five years is a long time, and I fear I have been a poor father, a cruel father, and perhaps even a stranger. Yet I write to you now, not out of obligation, but from the depths of my heart, which, I confess, has been a stormy place since the day you left.

The wind howls like a banshee as I write, and the sea, that old and restless beast, rages against the cliffs with a fury I have not seen in many a year. The lighthouse stands tall, as it always has, but I feel its bones creak beneath me, and I wonder if I, too, will be swallowed by the dark before the dawn.

I have been alone here for so long, Eleanor. The only company I have is the light, the wind, and the memory of you. I have kept the lamp burning, not just for the sailors, but for the hope that one day, you might return. I have told myself that perhaps, one day, you will come back to see the light that has guided me through the night, and I will be here to greet you.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

The lighthouse is a fortress of stone and iron, but even it cannot hold back the wrath of the elements. I fear I may not see morning.

Five years have passed since you left, and I have not heard a word from you. I have tried, Eleanor—tried with every ounce of my being. I have written, I have sent letters, and I have waited for your reply. But silence has been your answer, and I have come to accept it, though it wounds me more than I care to admit.

You were once my light, Eleanor—my greatest pride, my most precious treasure. I remember the day you were born, the way the sea seemed to pause in reverence, as if the very stars above had bent to witness the miracle. You were the light that I had longed for, the hope that had kept me standing through the darkest of nights. But now, that light is gone, and I am left in the darkness, alone, with only the sea and the wind for company.

I do not know what has changed between us. Perhaps you have found your own light elsewhere, or perhaps you have lost your way, as I have. I have not spoken of it, but I have felt it.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. The wind howls like a beast outside, and the sea roars as though it too is weeping. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I find myself unable to keep the truth from you.

I have been a lighthouse keeper for many years now, a solitary soul in a place where the world seems to forget us. I have watched the stars, the moon, and the endless waves, and in their quiet, I have thought of you often. I have not been the father you needed, nor the man you deserved. I have been a fool, and I have been a coward.

You left me when you were young, and I did not understand. I thought I could hold the light steady for you, but I could not hold the truth. I could not hold the love that had grown cold in my chest. I told myself that if I stayed, I would lose you to the sea, just as I had lost my wife. I told myself that distance was the only way to keep you safe.

But I was wrong.

I have not been safe. I have not been whole. And now, as the storm approaches, I find myself questioning whether I will survive it—or whether I have already been lost to the waves of my own making.

I write this with a hand that trembles not only from the cold but from the weight of years unspoken. Five years have passed since the last time I laid eyes upon you, and though the sea has never ceased its ceaseless rhythm, it has not been the same since you left.

**To My Dearest Clara,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not find you at all. The sea has been restless of late, and the wind howls like a wounded beast through the cracks of this old lighthouse. Five years have passed since you left, Clara. Five long, silent years. I’ve tried to write to you, but the letters always went unanswered. I suppose you never received them, or perhaps you chose not to. I can’t blame you for that. You were always the strong one, the one who could bear the weight of the world on her shoulders, while I—ah, I was too weak, too afraid to face what had come between us.

I remember the last time we spoke. You stood at the door of this lighthouse, your face pale, your eyes full of something I could not name. You said you needed to go, that you couldn’t stay. I tried to stop you, to beg you to stay, but you walked away without looking back. I’ve never forgiven myself for letting you go.

You were a child when you last stood in this lighthouse, your laughter echoing down the spiral staircase as you chased the seagulls that circled the cliffs. I remember the way your eyes sparkled with the promise of the world beyond these rocks, and I remember the way you left, with a suitcase and a heart full of dreams, and a mother who whispered, "You must go, my darling."

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a hand that trembles, not from the cold, but from the weight of words I have long kept locked within me. Five years have passed since you left this house, and though the days have worn on, the silence between us has grown heavier with each passing year. I have not spoken to you, not truly, and I fear that the silence may soon be all that remains.

The storm is coming. I can feel it in the air, a low, growling thing that whispers of the sea’s fury. The wind has been howling all night, and the lighthouse stands alone, a sentinel against the dark. I have lit the beacon, as I have done for thirty years, and though it burns with steady light, I cannot shake the feeling that this night may be the last one I see the dawn.

You were always a bright light in my life, Eleanor. Even when you were gone, your memory burned in my heart like the beacon that guides the lost. I remember the day you left—your eyes full of fire and sorrow, your words sharp as the sea breeze that cut through the morning air. You said I did not understand, that I was too bound by duty, too rigid in my ways. And perhaps you were right. I have spent my life watching the sea, not the people. **April 12th, 1897**

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a vengeful spirit through the cracks of this old tower. The sea has grown restless, and the sky is a canvas of black and silver, heavy with the promise of ruin. I fear the storm may not be kind to me, but if it does take me, I pray you will read this letter and know that I have not forgotten you.

Five long years have passed since you left, Eliza. Five years of silence, of letters left unanswered, of nights spent staring at the horizon, wondering if you ever looked back. I have tried to forget the last time we spoke, the way your eyes hardened when I dared to ask about your husband, or how you turned your back on me as if I were some stranger, not the father who raised you.

But I cannot forget. And I cannot forgive.

I have kept the lighthouse lit, as I always have, not because I believe in the sea’s mercy, but because I believe in the light. It has guided countless ships through the darkest nights, and I have stood here, alone, for thirty years. I have endured the cold, the loneliness, the weight of the world pressing down on my shoulders. But I have never truly felt the burden of my own heart—until now.

**The Lighthouse Keeper's Letter**  
*April 12th, 1897*  
*To My Dearest Eliza,*

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. The storm that rages outside my window is not the first such tempest I’ve weathered, but tonight feels different—more cruel, more relentless. The sea howls like a beast in pain, and the wind threatens to tear the very roof from this old stone tower. I pray the heavens will be merciful, and that I may see another dawn.

But I write not to beg for mercy, but to speak the words I have long kept locked away. You were once my greatest joy, Eliza. I remember the day you were born, the way the light from the lighthouse flickered in time with your first cries. You were my light in the darkness, my reason for keeping this lonely post. But time, as it often does, has a cruel way of changing things.

Five years have passed since you left. Five years since you told me I was too old, too stubborn, too *unloving* to be a father. You said I had no idea what it meant to care for a child. I did not argue. I did not beg. I simply closed the door to our home and let the silence settle in.

I have not spoken to you since. And I have not written. Not even a single letter

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

It is with a heart heavy with years of silence and sorrow that I take up this quill. The wind howls like a wounded beast beyond these stone walls, and the sea, in its fury, seems to echo the storm of my soul. I write this not in hope that you will read it, but in the hope that, should the heavens open and the sea claim me, my words may find their way to you, carried by the wind or by the tides.

Five years have passed since the last time our eyes met. Five years of silence, of unanswered letters, of memories that linger like the scent of salt on the air. I have tried, Eleanor, I have tried to reach you, to explain the things I could not say, to apologize for the things I could not undo. But you have turned your back, and I have not had the courage to turn mine.

I have been a lighthouse keeper for many years now, a solitary sentinel standing guard over the restless sea. It is a duty I have taken to heart, a calling that has given me purpose in the long, lonely nights. But tonight, as the storm gathers strength and the sky darkens with an unnatural fury, I find myself questioning whether this duty is the same as the one I once held for you.

I have always believed in the light—both the beacon that guides the lost and the light of love that should never be extinguished. But love

**April 12th, 1893**  
**Lighthouse, Isle of Skye**

My Dearest Eliza,

I write this with trembling hands and a heart that aches with the weight of silence. Five years have passed since the last time we spoke, and though the miles between us have grown, it is the miles of our hearts that have stretched into an abyss I fear I may never cross.

I know you do not wish to hear from me. You have made that clear, in your letters and in your silence. But tonight—tonight, the storm is coming. A tempest such as this island has not seen in a generation, and I fear the sea will not be kind. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the waves lash against the cliffs with a fury that makes me fear for my life.

I have spent my years in this lonely tower, watching the sky and the sea, and I have learned to read the signs. The moon is waning, the air is thick with the scent of rain, and the clouds churn like a beast in torment. I have lit the lamp, as I have done for thirty years, but tonight, I feel its light is but a whisper against the fury of the world.

You were always the daughter I wished for—a bright flame in a world of shadows. I tried to guide you, to teach you the ways of the sea, the patience of the storm, the stillness of

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast outside these walls. The sea roars in protest, and the sky is a bruised canvas of black and green, as if the heavens themselves have turned their back on us. I’ve spent the evening watching the waves, their fury mounting with every passing hour, and I find myself wondering if this is how the world ends—or if it is simply the world’s way of reminding us that we are small, fragile things, caught between the sky and the sea.

Five years, Eliza. Five years since you left this place, and I have not spoken to you. Not a word. Not a letter. Not even a single note slipped under the door. I have not heard your voice, nor felt your presence, and yet I have carried you with me every day. You are the ghost that haunts my nights, the shadow that lingers in my thoughts. I have tried to forget you, but memory is a cruel thing, and it has never left me.

I do not know why you left. I do not know if you still remember the days we spent together here, when the lighthouse was our world, and the sea our only companion. You were a child then, but you had a fire in your heart, a determination that made me proud. And yet, I watched you grow distant, as if the very air between us had turned to ice.

**The Lighthouse, Dunmore Head**  
**October 12th, 1889**  

My Dearest Eliza,  

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years. The wind howls like a banshee, and the sea roars as if it would swallow the very earth. Tonight, the storm is unlike any I have seen in my long years of tending this light. The waves rise like mountains, and the sky is a canvas of black and fury. I fear I may not see the dawn.

But I write not for my own sake, but for yours. For you, my daughter, whom I have not seen in five long years. Five years, Eliza. Five years since you left this house, this life, and this father behind you. I have not spoken your name in all that time, and I have not written to you. But tonight, the storm has made me feel the weight of silence like never before.

I know you do not want to hear from me. You have your own life, your own path, and I have been a fool to think that I could ever understand it. You were young then, and I was old, and I did not know how to speak the words that might have kept you from walking away. I have spent those years in regret, and in the quiet of this lighthouse, I have been forced to confront the truth: I failed you.

Five years have passed since you left. Five years since I last saw your face, and longer since your voice has filled this lonely house. I have tried to forget, to let time ease the pain, but it does not. It only grows colder with each passing day.

You were always so quick to leave, my dear. You called this life a prison, and I suppose, in a way, it was. But I did not know how to let you go. I thought, if I stayed, if I kept the light burning, perhaps you would return. Perhaps you would see that I am not the man you once thought I was.

I have not spoken to you in years, but tonight—tonight, the storm is coming. I have heard the signs in the wind, the way the sea has been restless, the way the sky has darkened as if the heavens themselves are weeping. I fear that this night may be my last. I have been a lighthouse keeper for thirty years, and I have seen many storms, but this one... it feels different. It feels like the end of something.

I have spent so many nights staring into the darkness, hoping to see a flicker of light, a sign that you were still out there, still living, still remembering.

I have spent many a night in this lighthouse, watching the waves crash against the shore, but never have I felt so alone as I do now, with the silence of your absence pressing upon me like the weight of the sea.

But then you grew up. You grew away. And with each passing year, the distance between us grew wider, until it became a chasm I could not cross.

**April 12th, 1897**  
**The Lighthouse, Isle of Skye**  
**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not find you at all. I’ve spent the past five years writing this, and every time I began, the words would not come. I would close the letter, set it aside, and return to my duties by the light. But now, as the wind howls and the sea roars like a beast in pain, I feel the weight of silence pressing upon me, and I must speak, if only to the sea and the wind, and to the ghost of the daughter I once knew.

Five years. That is how long it has been since you left. Five years since you walked out of our home, your face pale as the moon, your eyes filled with a fire I could not understand. I remember the day as if it were yesterday: the rain falling in sheets, the sky bruised with storm clouds, and you standing at the door, your hand on the latch, as if deciding whether to stay or to go. I tried to reach for you, but you turned away, and the door slammed shut behind you. That was the last time I saw you.
